    "internal_reasoning": "The artwork presented is a contemporary diptych featuring two large-scale portraits of a female subject, displayed in what appears to be a minimalist, curated interior setting. The two panels exhibit a striking color contrast: the left panel is dominated by cool, deep blue and violet tones, while the right panel utilizes warmer, reddish-pink hues. The subject matter is identical or near-identical, suggesting a study in lighting, color grading, or digital manipulation. \n\nVisually, the works appear to be backlit photographic prints (lightboxes) or high-resolution digital displays, evidenced by the visible power cables trailing from the bottom of the frames to the mid-century modern cabinet below. The surface has a luminous quality that distinguishes it from traditional oil or acrylic painting, pointing towards media art or fine art photography. The aesthetic is hyper-real yet ethereal, with a softness that is characteristic of digital post-processing or potentially AI-assisted generation, placing it firmly in the realm of post-internet art or contemporary digital photography.\n\nThe condition appears to be mint/pristine, with no visible damage to the frames or the image surface. The framing is simple, thin, and black, which is standard for contemporary gallery presentation, ensuring the focus remains on the image.\n\nThe primary challenge in valuation is the complete lack of attribution. There are no visible signatures, edition numbers, or gallery labels. In the art market, the artist's name is the primary driver of value. Without it, the work must be appraised based on its decorative merit, technical execution, and size. \n\nComparing this to the market for emerging contemporary photographers and digital artists (e.g., artists working with C-prints, lightboxes, or screen-based installations), a diptych of this apparent size (estimated roughly 30x40 inches or larger per panel) would typically enter the market in the low-to-mid four figures. If this were by a known artist like Gottfried Helnwein (for the hyperrealism) or a digital artist like Petra Cortright, the value would be exponentially higher ($20,000+). However, treating this as an unattributed work by an emerging artist, the value is driven by the cost of production (large scale lightboxes are expensive to produce) and its aesthetic appeal as a statement piece. The pairing with the vintage cabinet suggests a high-end interior design context, which supports a valuation higher than mass-market decor but lower than blue-chip fine art.\n\nTherefore, the valuation reflects a 'fair market value' for a high-quality, unattributed contemporary lightbox diptych sold through a reputable gallery or auction house specializing in emerging art.",

parsed.internal_reasoning:
The artwork presented is a contemporary diptych featuring two large-scale portraits of a female subject, displayed in what appears to be a minimalist, curated interior setting. The two panels exhibit a striking color contrast: the left panel is dominated by cool, deep blue and violet tones, while the right panel utilizes warmer, reddish-pink hues. The subject matter is identical or near-identical, suggesting a study in lighting, color grading, or digital manipulation. 

Visually, the works appear to be backlit photographic prints (lightboxes) or high-resolution digital displays, evidenced by the visible power cables trailing from the bottom of the frames to the mid-century modern cabinet below. The surface has a luminous quality that distinguishes it from traditional oil or acrylic painting, pointing towards media art or fine art photography. The aesthetic is hyper-real yet ethereal, with a softness that is characteristic of digital post-processing or potentially AI-assisted generation, placing it firmly in the realm of post-internet art or contemporary digital photography.

The condition appears to be mint/pristine, with no visible damage to the frames or the image surface. The framing is simple, thin, and black, which is standard for contemporary gallery presentation, ensuring the focus remains on the image.

The primary challenge in valuation is the complete lack of attribution. There are no visible signatures, edition numbers, or gallery labels. In the art market, the artist's name is the primary driver of value. Without it, the work must be appraised based on its decorative merit, technical execution, and size. 

Comparing this to the market for emerging contemporary photographers and digital artists (e.g., artists working with C-prints, lightboxes, or screen-based installations), a diptych of this apparent size (estimated roughly 30x40 inches or larger per panel) would typically enter the market in the low-to-mid four figures. If this were by a known artist like Gottfried Helnwein (for the hyperrealism) or a digital artist like Petra Cortright, the value would be exponentially higher ($20,000+). However, treating this as an unattributed work by an emerging artist, the value is driven by the cost of production (large scale lightboxes are expensive to produce) and its aesthetic appeal as a statement piece. The pairing with the vintage cabinet suggests a high-end interior design context, which supports a valuation higher than mass-market decor but lower than blue-chip fine art.

Therefore, the valuation reflects a 'fair market value' for a high-quality, unattributed contemporary lightbox diptych sold through a reputable gallery or auction house specializing in emerging art.
key_factors: Diptych format with contrasting color palettes (cool vs. warm)
Medium appears to be backlit print or digital display (lightbox)
Pristine condition and professional gallery-style framing
Lack of artist attribution or provenance significantly limits value
Strong decorative and contemporary aesthetic appeal
